subject: Chemistry
solution:
To find the temperature at which $k_1 = k_2$,
set the rate constant equations equal: $10^6 e^{-30000/T} = 10^4 e^{-24000/T}$.
Dividing both sides by $10^4$: $100 = e^{-24000/T + 30000/T} = e^{6000/T}$.
Taking natural logarithm: $\ln(100) = \frac{6000}{T}$.
Since $\ln(100) = 2\ln(10) = 2 \times 2.303 = 4.606$: $4.606 = \frac{6000}{T}$.
Solving for T: $T = \frac{6000}{4.606} = 1302.5$ K.
Rounding to the nearest integer gives T = 1303 K.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Reaction : 2 A+B2⟶2AB

As the reaction is elementary, the rate of reaction is

r=K.[A]2 B2

on reducing the volume by a factor of 3, the concentrations of A and B2 will become 3 times and hence, the rate becomes 32×3=27 times of initial rate.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Given: $A_1 = A_2$, $E_{a1} = E_{a2} + 20$ kJ/mol, T = 300 K, R = 8.3 J K⁻¹ mol⁻¹
Using Arrhenius equation: $k = Ae^{-E_a/RT}$
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{e^{-E_{a2}/RT}}{e^{-E_{a1}/RT}} = e^{(E_{a1} - E_{a2})/RT}$
$E_{a1} - E_{a2} = 20$ kJ/mol = 20000 J/mol
$\frac{k_2}{k_1} = e^{\frac{20000}{8.3 \times 300}} = e^{\frac{20000}{2490}}$
$\ln\frac{k_2}{k_1} = \frac{20000}{2490} = 8.03 \approx 8$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
From the given graph, the plot of half-life $t_{1/2}$ versus initial concentration $[A]_0$ is a straight line passing through the origin.

This indicates that $t_{1/2}$ is directly proportional to $[A]_0$, which means $t_{1/2} \propto [A]_0$.

Therefore, the ratio $\dfrac{t_{1/2}}{[A]_0}$ is constant.

Using the coordinates from the graph, we get:

$\dfrac{240}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{x}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$

Solving for $x$:

$x = \dfrac{240 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$

$x = 60 \times 1.5 = 90$

The value of $x$ is $90$.

Answer: $90$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For $A \xrightarrow{K_1} B$
$\ln(2) = \dfrac{E_{a_1}}{R} \left[ \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{500} \right]$
$E_{a_1} = \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{2}$
$E_{a_2} = \dfrac{E_{a_1}}{2} = \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{4}$
$(K_1)_{\text{at 500 K}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{2}$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 500 K}} = \ln 2$
Now for $C \xrightarrow{K_2} D$
$\ln \left[ \dfrac{(K_2)_{\text{at 500K}}}{(K_2)_{\text{at 300K}}} \right] = \left( \dfrac{\ln 2 \times R \times 1500}{4} \right) \times \dfrac{1}{R} \times \left[ \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{500} \right]$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 300 K}} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.49$
$(K_2)_{\text{at 300 K}} = 4.9 \times 10^{-1}$.
So, answer is 5.

For a first order reaction:
 ∴k=ln2t1/2=ln215 min

⇒y=xe-kt

=xe-ln215t    ....(1)

Similarly For B:
 [B]t=[B]0e-kt

⇒y=4xe-ln25t      ....(2)
Equating equation(1) and (2) we get:

⇒xe-ln215t=4xe-ln25t

⇒t×ln 25-ln 215=ln 4

⇒t×ln215-115=2 ln2

⇒etln 25-ln 215=4

⇒t=15 min

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2$
$\begin{array}{llll}\text { At } \mathrm{t}=0 & 50 & 0 & 0 \\ \text { At } \mathrm{t}=50 \mathrm{~min} & 50-\mathrm{p}_1 & 2 \mathrm{p}_1 & \frac{\mathrm{p}_1}{2}\end{array}$
Total pressure at $50$ minutes
$\begin{aligned}
&=50-\mathrm{p}_1+2 \mathrm{p}_1+\frac{\mathrm{p}_1}{2}=87.5 \\
&50+\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1}{2}=87.5 \\
&\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1}{2}=37.5 \\
&\therefore \quad \mathrm{p}_1=\frac{37.5 \times 2}{3}=25
\end{aligned}$
At $\mathrm{t}=100 \mathrm{~min} \quad 50-\mathrm{p}_2 \quad 2 \mathrm{p}_2 \quad \frac{\mathrm{p}_2}{2}$
$50$ minutes is half life period
For $100$ minutes i.e. for $2$ half lives $50-p_2=12.5$
$\therefore \mathrm{p}_2=37.5 \mathrm{~mm} \text { of } \mathrm{Hg}$
Total pressure at $100$ minutes
$=50-\mathrm{p}_2+2 \mathrm{p}_2+\frac{\mathrm{p}_2}{2}$
$=50+\frac{3 \mathrm{p}_2}{2}=50+\frac{3}{2} \times 37.5$
$=50+56.25$
$=106.25 \mathrm{~mm}$ of $\mathrm{Hg}$
